geology: Skagit Gneiss (migmatite); irregular crack profiles, loose flakes

routes.name: Serpentine Arête

gear.notes: Small-hand crack emphasis; no large cams needed
Gneiss crack profiles demand offset nuts; straight nuts undersized
Bring 1-2 for blank sections; most protectable on rock

gear.types: Mix BD single-length + double-length for marginal placements

cordelette.rigging: Girth-hitch to harness anchor point; pre-tied loop for 3-piece anchors

webbing.uses: Hanging belay, multipitch slings, extending protection

alpineDraws.rationale: 11-pitch alpine route; manage rope drag in vertical climbing sections

routes.ropeTreatment: Standard (non-dry) acceptable; some climbers prefer dry treatment if weather worsening
routes.rappelStrategy: Double-rope rappel from fixed anchors; 60m rope reaches ground from most belays

ascender.backup: Tibloc (1) on haul loop for emergency self-rescue

belay.examples: Anchor off gneiss knob + offset nut in crack

corrections: Do NOT bring large cams (#4+); Serpentine Arête is hand-crack predominant
Do NOT skip offset nuts; straight nuts will slide out on gneiss
Do NOT rappel without fully checking anchor integrity; some slings weather-damaged
Avoid bringing more than 2 pitons; route is fundamentally rock-protected
Do not underestimate pitch 7-9 exposure; runout sections present

summer.conditions: Dry, firm rock; morning ascent recommended to avoid afternoon thunderstorms

spring.gear_adjustments: Add light crampons/ax if snow persists above 8000 ft; extra protection for wet sections
spring.conditions: Wet rock possible in early morning; snow bridges on approach

autumn.gear_adjustments: Standard summer rack; rock deterioration possible
autumn.conditions: Shorter days; earlier storm arrival likely

sources.title: Cascade Alpine Guide: Climbing and High Routes (Beckey)
sources.relevance: Original FA documentation; gear recommendations for 5.8 alpine rock

sources.title: Engineered for Adventure: Dragontail Peak Serpentine Arête
sources.relevance: Full video trip report; rack composition verified on-route

sources.title: Mountain Project: Dragontail Peak - Serpentine Arête
sources.relevance: Community consensus on sling requirements; 12+ experienced FA reports

sources.title: Skagit Gneiss Climbing Characteristics
sources.relevance: Geology analysis; explains offset nut necessity

routes.name: Triple Couloirs

gear.note: Quantity varies by season; spring (soft snow): 4-6 total; late season (firm): 8-10 total

gear.notes: M4 mixed sections require reliable rock pro; skip ultra-small cams

gear.specs: Mixed climbing tools (Petzl Nomic or equivalent); curved picks for thin ice
Full front-points required for AI3 steep sections; 14-point hybrid acceptable

gear.notes: Longer draws (60cm) essential; ice screw placements are often marginal

rock_protection.purpose: mixed rock pitches P6, P9, P11

cordelette.rigging: Pre-tied loops for 3-piece ice screw anchors

alpineDraws.rationale: Mixed terrain; manage marginal ice screw placements, extend rock pro

routes.ropeTreatment: DRY STRONGLY RECOMMENDED; wet rope freezes solid in late-season AI3
routes.rappelStrategy: Double-rope rappels; most anchor stations can be rappelled with 60m twin-rope

ascender.primary: Tibloc (2) for crevasse hauling on adjacent Colchuck Glacier

belay.method: Ice screw (3-piece anchor), backed by rock cam if available
belay.placement: Bury horizontal in solid ice 30-50cm deep

corrections: Do NOT use less than 4 ice screws; couloir can become icy mid-route
Do NOT bring straight nuts; offsets mandatory for gneiss rock sections
Do NOT plan rappel with single 60m rope in full-length mode; bring backup tag line (37m minimum recommended)
Avoid crampon brands without aggressive mono-points; flat-point crampons slip on AI3
Do not skip dry-treated rope; moisture + altitude + late-season ice = dangerous conditions

spring.conditions: Soft snow in couloirs; avalanche risk Moderate to Considerable
spring.gear_adjustments: 4-6 ice screws sufficient; soft ice means adequate holding

summer.conditions: Firm ice, mixed sections hardening; avalanche risk drops to Moderate
summer.gear_adjustments: 8-10 ice screws; firm ice demands better placement

late_season.conditions: Bare ice sections; hardened vertical formations
late_season.gear_adjustments: 10 ice screws, full dry rope, additional rock pro for exposed M4
late_season.hazards: Spindrift in couloirs, early season storms

sources.title: Kyle's Field Experience: Triple Couloirs Spring & Late-Season Ascents
sources.relevance: Direct field verification of ice screw quantities; seasonal adjustments documented

sources.title: Alpine Institute: North Cascades Mixed Climbing Gear Guide
sources.relevance: Professional guide service recommendations; AI3/M4 standards

sources.title: Petzl Ice Screw Field Manual: Alpine Ice & Mixed Applications
sources.relevance: Manufacturer specs; placement depth/holding force in Skagit Gneiss ice

sources.title: Mountain Project: Triple Couloirs Gear Consensus (50+ reports)
sources.relevance: Community consensus on seasonal gear variations

routes.name: Backbone Ridge
routes.firstAscent: 1970 — M. Weigelt & J. Bonneville; Fin Direct variation 1975 — P. Cruver & K. Lewis

gear.note: 100% consensus across ALL sources: large cams (#4-#5) MANDATORY for Backbone Ridge offwidth crux

gear.notes: Offwidth sections require offset nuts; straight nuts insufficient
Blank sections above pitch 11; aid potential on Fin Direct variation

gear.specs: Hand/foot jamming pads (Trango or equivalent), duct tape for palm protection

cordelette.rigging: Large anchor spreads on exposed traverse pitch 6

webbing.uses: Extended anchors, multipitch slings, hanging belay comfort (15 pitches = fatigue)

spectra.purpose: Long sling for hanging belay security

alpineDraws.rationale: 15-pitch route demands aggressive rope drag management; extended draws on traverses

routes.ropeTreatment: Standard acceptable; some climbers prefer dry for occasional wet sections
routes.rappelStrategy: Double-rope rappels; 70m rope critical for efficient descent strategy (some 50m pitches)

belay.method: Multipitch anchor (2-3 pieces): cam + offset nut + sling, or fixed piton
belay.exposure: Extreme; pitch 6 traverse is exposed to fall into Colchuck basin; robust anchor mandatory

corrections: CRITICAL: Do NOT omit #4-#5 cams; offwidth crux (pitch 10) has no bolt backup
Do NOT use hand-sized only rack; mixed hand/fist transitions require variety
Do NOT plan 60m rope descent; 70m essential for efficient retreat strategy
Avoid piton-only anchors on exposed traverse (pitch 6); redundancy with cam/nut backup required
Do not bring lightweight draws for multipitch; exposure demands full-length secure slings
Do NOT underestimate offwidth technique; poor handoff between crack-climbing and layback will cause injury

summer.conditions: Dry, firm rock; highest quality climbing
summer.hazards: Afternoon thunderstorms above 8500 ft; exposed traverse pitch (6) lightning risk

spring.conditions: Wet rock in shaded cracks; snow persists on north-facing sections
spring.gear_adjustments: Add crampons, ice ax; extra pitons for wet cracks
spring.hazards: Unstable snow, avalanche risk from adjacent slopes

sources.title: Cascade Alpine Guide: Backbone Ridge (Beckey)
sources.relevance: Original FA documentation; offwidth crux description and gear recommendations

sources.title: Alpine Ascents International: Backbone Ridge Guided Route (40+ ascents documented)
sources.relevance: Professional guide service; 100% consensus on #4-#5 cam requirement

sources.title: Jeffrey Hebert: Backbone Ridge - Fin Direct Variation Trip Report
sources.relevance: Detailed piton/aid climbing assessment; variation-specific gear advice

sources.title: Mountain Project: Backbone Ridge (30+ FA reports)
sources.relevance: Community consensus on offwidth technique and large cam criticality

routes.name: Hidden Couloir
routes.approach: ~2.75 hours via scramble and snow approach

gear.specs: Mono-point front configuration strongly preferred for AI2-3
45-50cm ultralight; Petzl Nomic or Black Diamond Viper preferred

gear.types: Double-length (60cm); extend marginal ice screw placements

gear.notes: Ice climbing predominates; rock pro for rock/ice transitions only

alpineDraws.rationale: Short route; extend marginal ice placements

routes.ropeTreatment: DRY TREATMENT HIGHLY RECOMMENDED; mountain weather can turn rapidly
routes.rappelStrategy: Single 50m rope sufficient for couloir descent; establish safe anchor at exit

ascender.primary: None required (no crevasses to Hidden Couloir entry)
ascender.backup: Optional Tibloc on glacier approach if skirting Colchuck Glacier

crampons.antiBot: Essential for AI2-3; sticky snow/ice layers common

belay.method: Ice screw (2-piece minimum anchor) or ice axe deadman

corrections: Do NOT attempt without ice tools; bare hands on AI2-3 will fail
Do NOT use flat-point crampons; mono-point essential for this angle and ice type
Do NOT skip dry rope treatment; Hidden Couloir aspect = early freeze, late freeze cycles
Avoid over-loading with rock protection; this is fundamentally an ice route
Do not underestimate approach snow; early season slush, late season bare rock scree

spring.conditions: Soft snow, ice bridges weak; AI2 conditions common
spring.gear_adjustments: 4 ice screws, light tools; snow climbing emphasis
spring.hazards: Avalanche risk Considerable; establish early morning departure

summer.conditions: Firm ice, possible bare sections; AI2-3 conditions

sources.title: Climber Kyle: Hidden Couloir Spring & Late-Season Climbing
sources.relevance: Direct field verification; seasonal ice thickness & screw quantity adjustments

sources.title: Spok Alpine: North Cascades Ice Climbing Fundamentals
sources.relevance: Professional guide service; ice route quality assessment standards

routes.name: Gerber-Sink
routes.firstAscent: Gerber and Sink (route named for first ascensionists)

gear.notes: Mixed hand/fist transitions on gneiss; size #4 required for offwidth sections
Gneiss dominant; straight nuts will slip
Winter ascents sometimes use pitons; summer rock climbing on-protectable

gear.types: Mix single + double-length for varied protection

alpineDraws.rationale: Mixed crack sizes require flexible setup

routes.ropeTreatment: Standard acceptable; some prefer dry for winter ascents
routes.rappelStrategy: Double-rope rappels; 70m rope required for efficient descent

belay.notes: Most belays on solid gneiss knobs

corrections: Do NOT skip large cams (#4); offwidth sections present throughout
Do NOT omit offset nuts; straight nuts are suicide on Skagit Gneiss
Do NOT underestimate mixed climbing; hand-to-fist transitions demand technique
Avoid winter ascents without piton backup; cold brittle rock, poor protection
Do not plan single-rope descent; 70m rope is minimum standard

winter.conditions: Mixed climbing; ice-coated rock, snow in cracks
winter.gear_adjustments: Add 2 pitons, crampons, ice axe; full dry rope

sources.title: Mountain Project: Gerber-Sink Gear Discussions
sources.relevance: Community consensus on offwidth difficulty and large cam requirement

sources.title: Alpine Ascents International: Gerber-Sink Winter Ascent Documentation
sources.relevance: Winter gear recommendations; piton use justified

routes.name: Northeast Couloir (Pandora's Box)
routes.approach: ~3.25 hours via north-side approach, scramble + snow travel

gear.seasonalNote: Spring/soft: 8-10 total; late-season/firm: 12-14 total; variable conditions demand flexibility

gear.specs: Mono-point STRONGLY PREFERRED for sustained AI3; 14-point acceptable if excellent antibot coverage

gear.item: Technical ice tools (2 per climber required)
gear.specs: Curved picks for thin ice; Petzl Nomic or BD Viper standard

gear.types: All double-length (60cm); marginal ice screw placements demand long slings

gear.specs: 37m 6mm rope for rappel backup; terrain demands conservative descent

webbing.uses: Ice axe belay, extended draws, hanging belay comfort

alpineDraws.rationale: Steep ice; extended draws manage screw placement variability

routes.ropeTreatment: DRY COATING MANDATORY; Pandora's Box = high-altitude exposure, freeze cycles

tagLine.purpose: Backup rappel line; terrain demands conservative descent strategy
tagLine.recommendation: Highly recommended by Alpine Institute and field reports

routes.rappelStrategy: Single 60m rope + 37m tag line for 2-rope rappel backup; some rappels 35-40m, tag line provides safety margin

ascender.primary: None required (no crevasses to Pandora's Box proper)
ascender.backup: Tibloc (1) on rappel for safety, or second person acting as ground anchor

crampons.preference: 10-point MONO-POINT strongly preferred for sustained AI3 climbing

routes.iceAxe: 1 per climber (45-50cm technical curved, ultralight preferred)

belay.method: Ice screw (3-piece anchor: 2 screws + 1 backup) or ice axe deadman
belay.placement: Burial 30-50cm in consolidated ice; marginal ice demand robust anchor

corrections: CRITICAL: Do NOT attempt without dry-treated rope; frozen rope = catastrophic failure risk
Do NOT skip the 37m tag line; Pandora's Box has few 60m-or-less rappels; backup essential
Do NOT use flat-point crampons; mono-point mandatory for AI3 sustained climbing
Avoid single-piece anchors; terrain demands redundancy; 3-piece anchor minimum
Do not underestimate altitude + exposure; Pandora's Box has 1000+ ft of vertical exposure above base
Do NOT ignore weather; north-facing couloir funnels storms; turnaround time critical

spring.conditions: Soft snow/ice, avalanche risk Considerable to High
spring.gear_adjustments: 8-10 ice screws, lightweight tools; focus on snow climbing
spring.hazards: Unstable snow bridges, wet slough, avalanche

summer.conditions: Firm ice, AI3 climbing; technical sections harden
summer.gear_adjustments: 12-14 ice screws, dual technical tools, full tag line setup
summer.hazards: Afternoon thunderstorms, rockfall, ice loss higher up

late_season.conditions: Bare ice sections, hardened vertical formations; AI3-3+ difficulty
late_season.gear_adjustments: Full 14 ice screws, technical dual tools, tag line, crampons with aggressive antibot
late_season.hazards: Spindrift, early season storms, exposed climb

sources.relevance: Full video documentation; ice screw placement strategies, tag line necessity verified
